title: The End of Evangelion Just be
display_title: The End of Evangelion Just be
wikitext:
[]: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by StarShaper7 » Mon Sep 22, 2014 3:18 pm I've seen NGE twice and EoE maybe about 7 times. My thoughts might change with another full viewing, but here's what I'm thinking at the moment. The only real experience and knowledge I have of writing formal analyses is from high school English and reading stuff on the internet (great source, I know). I'm majoring in a completely different subject (at the moment), so there are definitely people better equipped than me to write something like this. I'm not really doing anything in-depth, that would take too much time and effort, both being resources that would be better used on something else. But I just had to get my thoughts out on EoE. So, here goes. EDIT I re-wrote the first paragraph. Here's the original version: SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Sep 22, 2014 5:01 pm StarShaper7 wrote: We all know EoE is a heavily masculine film.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Mon Sep 22, 2014 5:02 pm It's all lingams and yonis.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by Atropos » Mon Sep 22, 2014 5:55 pm Reichu wrote: When I think "masculine", The End of Evangelion doesn't even bother getting in my mental cue for consideration, because it already knows it doesn't remotely qualify.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Sep 22, 2014 6:23 pm Atropos wrote: It may not be "manly", but I've never seen such a thorough evaluation of the psyche of the modern male.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Sep 22, 2014 6:29 pm Reichu wrote: When I think "masculine", The End of Evangelion doesn't even bother getting in my mental cue for consideration, because it already knows it doesn't remotely qualify.

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Mon Sep 22, 2014 6:38 pm I quite agree with Reichu; Shinji is pretty good as far as we're going to get with a "normal" person being equally relatable to all ages and sexes. He's on the cusp between adult and childhood; he's more feminine than he is masculine, in attitude and traits; he can be aggressive but he's usually passive, and he's no more physically capable than his female counter-part. I think Shinji's feminine qualities nicely counter his "birth right" - and all the shafting that comes with it. He's as split as it's going to get, without an operation or a birth defect. No, I do not think Evangelion is a masculine show - as much as Nadia is a feminine one.

[]:             Quote Post by StarShaper7 » Mon Sep 22, 2014 11:17 pm I think I should clarify. I think Evangelion is masculine because it's focused on a male protagonist whose masculinity is in question. It is because he fails to meet the criteria for masculinity that causes his inner turmoil. He isn't able to "take responsibility" like so many male anime protagonists do. I see the Komm Susser Tod sequence as being like a man who has been repressing himself for so long finally letting it all out, in one big violent and sexual blast. The MPReis impaling themselves with spears of longinus, moaning in ecstasy, Eva-01 as the tree of life stabbing through Rei's third-eye-vagina, etc. Shinji struggles to understand the women around him, apparent in the hell train sequence in which he accuses the three women of "keeping things ambiguous." We hear those rejection lines from women in that montage. We don't see anyone else's instrumentality, because the film becomes about Shinji's problems. I think the message of EoE is universal, but this letter (let's call it a letter) is written by a man with a specific recipient in mind, the otaku. And I mean the ones that withdraw from reality to the comfortable illusion of fictional worlds and their eternally faithful, subservient (whether the character's personality matches up is irrelevant, they'll just print her in a sexualized and inviting manner on a dakimakura), made-for-only-them women. And thanks for replying to this one, guys. The last topic I started got zero replies. But I guess that was my fault for writing about something obscure and completely unrelated to Eva. But what do you all think about the part that comes after me calling it masculine?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Sep 22, 2014 11:31 pm Calling it "masculine" probably isn't the best way to get your idea across, and it also comes off as needlessly reductive. (The female element of the film is so huge it's literally penetrating the stratosphere.) I don't disagree with most of the rest of what you've said, but I don't think it works well as a rigid framework for the entire movie.

[]:             Quote Post by Compiling_Autumn » Tue Sep 23, 2014 12:34 am I think this is a strong analysis. That's not to say that there aren't other ways of looking through it, but I think the masculine/sexual analysis is one of the most important angles to EoE. I would go one step further and say that EoE reveals to the audience how profound sexual frustration is as a driving force for these characters. Part of what makes is so affecting, to me, is how unafraid the movie is to strip its characters down and show us what was going on inside.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Tue Sep 23, 2014 3:20 am Well, debate about the "masculinity" aside, I think that this analysis is spot on, especially the part about not blindly accepting the standards of society, since Anno himself said in an interview that he doesn't find himself in the current Japanese society. (at least back in the 90's)

[]:             Quote Post by StarShaper7 » Wed Sep 24, 2014 12:43 pm Thanks, Compiling_Autumn and ElMariachi. Reichu wrote: Calling it "masculine" probably isn't the best way to get your idea across, and it also comes off as needlessly reductive. (The female element of the film is so huge it's literally penetrating the stratosphere.)

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Wed Sep 24, 2014 7:32 pm A better way to put this might not be to classify the entire film as masculine, but instead make the statement of there existing a strong component which deals with the psyche of men, male emotion, and male ego, and what it means to step outside those components; one of the reasons most people entering the series take big smelly shits on Shinji is because he simply doesn't step inside the traditional male mold. In my head, and pretty much only in my head, there exists within EoE and NGE an underlying preoccupation about the conflation of male tradition (what men have been throughout history) and male progress (What men must become) as masculinity struggles to find a place in a generally feminine world. Gendo might represents the former, Shinji the latter. But it's reductive to simplify it to those two (characters) alone.

[]:             Quote Post by StarShaper7 » Wed Sep 24, 2014 9:58 pm Sgt. Griff wrote: In my head, and pretty much only in my head, there exists within EoE and NGE an underlying preoccupation about the conflation of male tradition (what men have been throughout history) and male progress (What men must become) as masculinity struggles to find a place in a generally feminine world.

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Thu Sep 25, 2014 1:44 am What people fail to witness when they view society is the middle. The top is obvious, the bottom is obvious, but the middle in which everybody lives is largely ignored. There are a lot of women in the middle, more than men. But, that is largely beside the point. in Evangelion Shinji is (essentially) without a father, lives with a woman, later on two, is commanded by women, experimented on by women, and he pilots alongside women, not to mention the main motivation towards Gendo's instrumentality is a woman. There is constant reminder of the mother throughout, and the only fathers were kinda dicks. Kaji stands out as a strong "manly" man, and it's one of the reasons he can convince Shinji to get in the robot and take down Zeruel. Maybe this world is not, but their world is, as Reichu pointed out, the feminine aspect is literally enough to pierce the heavens. What lies within is feminist meditation on masculinity and manliness in a post feminist world, which would hold even more true in a culture as traditional and as rigid as Japan's which even today struggles to embrace the new and move away from old traits like nationalism, sexism, and racism.

[]:             Quote Post by StarShaper7 » Sun Sep 28, 2014 2:20 am Sgt. Griff wrote: What people fail to witness when they view society is the middle. The top is obvious, the bottom is obvious, but the middle in which everybody lives is largely ignored. There are a lot of women in the middle, more than men. But, that is largely beside the point.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: Just be.             Quote Post by Ultrahuntr » Mon Sep 29, 2014 3:10 pm Reichu wrote: When I think "masculine", The End of Evangelion doesn't even bother getting in my mental cue for consideration, because it already knows it doesn't remotely qualify.
